That is a strong measure, and a god who once could do that, was no doubt capable of anything afterwards. The same idea, namely that Indra is greater than heaven and earth, is expressed in a less outrageous way by another poet, who says f that Indra is greater than heaven and earth, and that both together are only a half of Indra. Or again : } "The divine Dyaus bowed before Indra, before Indra the great Earth bowed with her wide spaces.' " At the birth of thy splendor Dyaus trembled, the Earth trembled for fear of thy anger." Thus, from one point of view, Heaven and Earth were the greatest gods, they were the parents of everything, and therefore of the gods also, such as Ind and others.

" But, from another point of view, every god that was considered as supreme at one time or other, must necessarily have made heaven and earth, must at all events be greater than heaven and earth, and thus the child became greateV than the father, aye, became the father of his father. Indra was not the only god that created heaven and earth. In one hymn || that creation is ascribed to Soma and Pushan, by no means very prominent characters ; in another ^ to Hira/^yagarbha (the golden germ) ; in another again to a god who is simply called Dhatn, the Creator, ** or Fijvakarman,tt the maker of all things.
Other gods, such as Mitra and Savitr^, names of Muir iv. 31, note. t Rig-veda VI. 30, i.

THE LESSONS OF THE VEDA. t*l% the sun, are praised for upholding Heaven and Earth, and the same task is sometimes performed by the old god Varu;/a * also.^ What I wish you to observe in all this is the perfect freedom with which these so-called gods or Devas are handled, and particularly the ease and naturalness with which now the one, now the other emerges as supreme out of this chaotic theogony. This is the peculiar character of the ancient Vedic religion, totally different both from the Polytheism and from the Monotheism as we see it in the Greek and the Jewish religions ; and if the Veda had taught us nothing else but this honotheistic phase, which must everywhere have preceded the more highly organized phase of Polytheism which we see in Greece, in Rome, and elsewhere, the study of the Veda would not have been in vain.

It may be quite true that the poetry of the Veda is neither beautiful, in our sense of the word, nor very profound ; but it is instructive. When we see those two giant spectres of Heaven and Earth on the background of the Vedic religion, exerting their influence for a time, and then vanishing before the light of younger and more active gods, we learn a lesson which it is well to learn, and which we can hardly learn anywhere else — the lesson how gods were made and unmade-^\iov^ the Beyond or the Infinite was named by different names in order to bring it near to the mind of man, to make it for a time comprehensible, until, when name after name had proved of no avail, a nameless God was felt to answer best the restless cravings of the human heart.

1 7 4 WNA T CAN INDIA TEA CH US f I shall next translate to you the hymn to which 1 referred before as addressed to the Rivers. If the Rivers are to be called deities at all, they belong to the class of terrestrial deities. But the reason why I single out this hymn is not so much because it throws new light on the theogonic process, but because it may help to impart some reality to the vague conceptions which we form to ourselves of the ancient Vedic poets and their surroundings. The rivers invoked are, as we shall see, the real rivers of the Punjab, and the poem shows a much wider geographical-horizon than we should expect from a mere village bard.* 1. " Let the poet declare, O Waters, your exceeding greatness, here in the seat ofVivasvat.t By seven and seven they have come forth in three courses, but the Sindhu (the Indus) exceeds all the other wandering rivers by her strength.

2. "Varu^^a dug out paths for thee to walk on, when thou rannest to the race. $ Thou proceedest t Vivasvat is a name of the sun, and the seat or home of Vivasvat can hardly be anything but the earth, as the home of the sun, or, in a more special sense, the place where a sacrifice is offered.

X I formerly translated yat vagan abhi adrava-^ tvam by *' when thou rannest for the prizes." Grassman had translated similarly. " When thou, O Sindhu, rannest to the prize of the battle," while Ludwig wrote, "When thou, O Sindhu, wast flowing on to greater powers." Va^a, cornected with vegeo, vigeo, vigil, wacker (see Curtius, Grundziige, No. i5Q),is one of the many difficult words in the Veda the general meaning of which maybe guessed, but in many places cannot yet be determined with certainty. Va^a occurs very frequently, both in the singular and the plural, and some of its meanings are clear enough. The Petersburg Dictionary gives the following list of them — swiftness, race, prize of race, gain, treasure, race-horse, etc. Here we perceive at once the difiiculty of tracing all these meanings back THE LESSONS OF THk VEDA, IJ3 6n a precipitous ridge of the earth, when thou art lord in the van of all the moving streams.
3. " The sound rises up to heaven above the earth ; she stirs up with splendor her endless power.* As from a cloud, the showers thunder forth, when the Sindhu comes roaring like a bull.

4. " To thee, O Sindhu, they (the other rivers) come as lowing mother-cows (run) to their young with their milk.f Like a king in battle thou leadest the two wings, when thou reachest the front of these downrushing rivers, 5. " Accept, O Ganga (Ganges), Yamuna (Jumna), Sarasvati (Sursuti), Sutudri (Sutlej), Parush;/i (Irato a common source, though it might be possible to begin with the meanings of strength, strife, contest, race, whether friendly or warlike, then to proceed to what is won in a race or in war, viz. booty, treasure, and lastly to take vaga/z in a more general sense of acquisitions, goods, even goods bestowed as gifts. We have a similar transition of meaning in the Greek aQAo?, contest for a prize, and aOXov, the prize of contest, reward, gift, while in the plural ra a6Aa stands again for contest, or even the place of combat. The Vedic va^mbhara may in fact be rendered bya6A.o<pd/3o5va^asati by dOXodvrrj. The transition from fight to prize is seen in passages such as : Rig-veda VI. 45, 12, va^an indra jravayyan tvaya ^eshma hilam dhdnam, " May we with thev help, O Indra, win the glorious fights the offered prize" (cf, dBXoBsvrj's).
Rig-veda VIII. 19. 18, te it va^ebhi>^ ^"gyu/^ mahat dhdnam, " They won great wealth by battles."

What we want for a proper understanding of our verse, are passages where we have, as here, a movement towards va^j in the plural. Such passages are few ; for instance; X. 53. 8, atra^ahama ye ajan ajeva/4 jivan vayam ut tarema abhi va^in, " Let us leave here those who were unlucky (the dead), and let us get up to lucky toils." No more probably meant here when the Sindhu is said to run towards her v%^s, that is her struggles, her fights, her race across the mountains with the other rivers, We find jubhram j-ushman II. 1 1* 4 ; and iyati with jushman IV. 17, I2»
t See Muir, Sanskrit Texts, v^ p. 344.
t ^ 6 ^^^ T CAN INDIA Tea cii us f vati, Ravi,) my praise ! ^ With the Asikni (Akesines) listen, O Marudv;/dha, t and with the Vitasta (Hydaspes, Behat) ; O Ar^ikiaya,^ listen with the Sushoma.

6. " First thou goest united with the Tnsh/ama on thy journey, with the Susartu, the Rasa (Ra;;/ha, Araxes || ? ), and the 5vett, — O Sindhn, with the Kubha (Kophen, Cabul river) to the Gomati (G-omal), with the Mehatnu to the Krumu (Kurum) — with whom thou proceedest together.
7. " Sparkling, bright, with mighty splendor she carries the water across the plains — the unconquered Sindhu, the quickest of the quick, like a beautiful mare — a sight to see.

8. " Rich in horses, in chariots, in garments, in gold, in booty,^ in wool, ** and in straw,f f the Sindhu, the Sushomo," Ludwig, " Asikni and Vitasto and MarudvWdha, with the Sushomo, hear us, O Ar^ikiyo," Grasmann. ^ t Marudvr/dho, a general name for a river. According to Roth the combined course of the Akesines and Hydaspes, before the junction with the Hydraotes according to Ludwig the river after the junction with Hydraotes. Zimmer (Altindisches Leben, p. 12) adopts Roth's Kiepert in his maps follow's Ludwig's opinion.
X According to Y^ska the Ar^kiy^ is the Vivien de Saint-Martain takes it for the country watered by the Suwan, the Soanos of Megasthenes.

Accordng to Yaska the Sushom^, is the Indus. Vivien de SaintMartin identifies it with the Suwan. Zimmer (1. c. p. 14) points out that in Arrian, Indica, iv. 12, there is a various reading Soamos for Soanos.

II Chips from a German Workshop, vol. i, p. 157, H Va^inivati is by no means an easy word. Hence all translators vary, and none settles the meaning. Muir translates, " yielding nutriment;" Zimmer, "having plenty of quick horses ;'* Ludwig, "like a strong mare.'' Va^n,no doubt, means a strong horse, a racer, butva^* ni never occurs in the Rig-veda in the sense of a mare, and the text is THE LESSONS OF THE VEDA, 177 handsome and young, clothes herself in sweet flowers.* 9. " The Sindhu has yoked her easy chariot with not va^inivat but va^nivati. If va^ni meant mare, we might translate rich in mares, but that would be a mere repetition after svajva, possessed of good hcrses. Va^'nivati is chiefly applied to Ushas, Sarasvati, and he-re to the river Sindhu. It is joined with va^ebhi/z, Rig-veda I. 3. 10, which if vao-ini meant mare, would mean *' rich in mares through horses." We also read, Rig-veda I. 48, 16, sam (na/z mimikshva) va^ai/^ va^nivati, which we can hardly translate by "give us horses, thou who art possessed of mares ; " nor, Rig-veda I. 92, 15, yiikshva hi va^inivati aj-van, " harness the horses , thou who art rich in mares." In most of the passages where vainivati occurs, the goddess thus addressed is represented as rich, and asked to bestow wealth, and I should therefore prefer to take va^ni, as a collective abstract noun, like tretini, in the sense of wealth, originally booty, and to translate vaoinivati simply by rich, a meaning well adapted to every passage where the word occurs.
\ Ur;zavati, rich in wool, probably refers to the flocks of sheep for which the northwest of India was famous. See Rig-veda I. 126, 7.

" rich in plants ; " Zimmer, " rich in water ; " Ludwig takes it as a proper name. Saya;za states that silama is a plant which is made into ropes. That the meaning of silamavati was forgotten at an early time we see by the Atharva-veda III. 12. 2, substituting snnr/tavati for silamavati, as preserved in the 6'ankhS,yana Gr/hyasutras, 3, 3, I think silama means straw, from whatever plant it may be taken, and this would be equally applicable to a j-ala, a house, a suth;za, a post and to the river Indus. It may have been, as Ludwig conjectures, an old .ocal name, and in that case it may possibly account for the na^e given in later times to the Suleiman range.

the Rig-veda. Saya^za explains it by nirguw^i and similar plants? but it is doubtful what plant is meant. Guwafa is the name of a grass, madhuvrzdh therefore may have been a plant such as sugarcane, that yielded a sweet juice, the Upper Indus being famous for sugar-cane; see Hiouen-thsang, II, p. 105. I take adhivaste with Roth in the sense '* she dresses herself," as we might say " the river is dressed in heather.'* Muir translates, " she traverses a land yielding sweetness ; " Zimmer, " she clothes herself in Madhuv^Mh ; " Ludwig, "the Silamavati throws herself into the increaser of the honey-sweet 178 IVHAl CAN INDIA TEACH US ?

horses , may she conquer prizes for us in the race. The greatness of her chariot is praised as truly great — that chariot which is irresistible, which has its own glory, and abundant strength."* This hymn does not sound perhaps very poetical, m our sense of the word ; yet if you will try to realise the thoughts of the poet who composed it, you will perceive that it is not without some bold and powerful conceptions.

Take the modern peasants, living in their villages by the side of the Thames, and you must admit that he would be a remarkable man who could bring himself to look on the Thames as a kind of general, riding at the head of many English rivers, and leading them on to a race or a battle. Yet it is easier to travel in England, and to gain a commanding view of the river-system of the country, than it was three thousand years ago to travel over India, even over that part of India which the poet of our hymn commands. He takes in at one swoop three great riversystems, or, as he calls them, three great armies of rivers — those flowing from the North- West into the Indus, those joining it from the North-East, and, in the distance, the Granges and the Jumnah with their tributaries. Look on the map and you will see how well these three armies are determined ; but our poet had no map — he had nothing but high mountains and sharp eyes to carry out his trigonometrical survey. Now I call a man, who for the first time dew." All this shows how little progress can be made in Vedic scholarship by merely translating either words or verses, without giving at the sama time a full justifieation ®f the meaning assigned t« every single word.
THE LESSONS CF THE VEDA. 1 79 could see those three marching armies of rivers, a poet.

The next thing that strikes one in that hymn — if hymn we must call it — is the fact that all these rivers, large and small, have their own proper names. That shows a considerable advance in civilised life, and it proves no small degree of coherence, or what the French call solidarity, between the tribes who had taken possession of Northren India. Most settlers call the river on whose banks they settle " the rive^y Of course there are many names for river. It may be called the runner *, the fertiliser, the roarer — or, with a little poetical metaphor, the arrow, the horse, the cow, the father, the mother, the watchman, the child,of the mountains. Many rivers had many names in different parts of their course, and it was only when communication between different settlements became more frequent, and a fixed terminology was felt to be a matter of necessity, that the rivers of a country were properly baptized and registered. All this had been gone through in India before our hymn became possible.

And now we have to consider another, to my mind most startling fact. We here have a number of names of the rivers of India, as they were known to one single poet, say about 1000 b. c. We then hear nothing of India till we come to the days of Alexander, and when we look at the names of the Indian rivers, represented as well as they could be by Alexander's companions, mere strangers in India, and by f ^* Among *h« Hottentots, the Kunene, ®kavang» and #range rivers, all have the name of Garib, i. e. the Runner." T>r. Theoph. Hahn. Cape Tinnes, July 11, 1S82.
zh: 172 我能從印度學到什麼？ 「你的父親和你的母親，都來自你自己的身體！」

這是一項大膽的舉動，而一位曾經能夠做到這點的提婆，無疑在之後具有無所不能的力量。同一觀念，亦即因陀羅偉大於天地，在另一位詩人筆下以較不過分的方式表達，他說因陀羅偉大於天地，兩者合在一起只不過是因陀羅的一半。或者再者：「神聖的特尤斯在因陀羅面前低眉順眼，在因陀羅面前偉大的大地與其寬闊的空間也低眉順眼。」「在汝光輝誕生之時，特尤斯為之顫抖，大地因懼怕汝怒火而為之顫抖。」如此，從某一觀點來看，天地乃是最偉大的提婆，它們是萬物之親，因此也是如因陀羅等諸提婆之親。

但是，從另一個角度來看，凡是在某個時期被視為至高的提婆，必然都曾創造過天地，無論如何都必須偉大於天地，因此子神就變得偉大於父神，甚至成為其父神的父神。因陀羅（Indra）並非唯一創造天地的提婆。在某一首讚歌中，創造被歸功於蘇摩（Soma）和普善（Pushan），這兩位絕非杰出的人物；在另一首中歸功於金胎（Hiranyagarbha）；在又另一首中歸功於一位簡稱為造主（Dhatr）的提婆，或稱為毗濕瓦羯摩（Viśvakarman），萬物的製造者。
其他提婆，如彌多羅（Mitra）和薩維特爾（Savitr），則名字見穆爾（Muir）第四卷第31頁注釋。†《梨俱吠陀》第六卷第30首讚歌第一節。

太陽以及諸如日輪的諸神，因其維繫天地而受到讚頌，同樣的任務有時也由古老的提婆神瓦魯那（Varu*a）執行。我希望你在這一切中觀察到的是，這些所謂的神祇或提婆（Devas）被處理時的完全自由，特別是其中現在這一個、現在那一個以一種輕鬆自然的方式從這混亂的神譜中浮現為至高者的情形。這正是古代吠陀宗教的獨特性格，與我們在希臘和猶太宗教中所見的多神教和一神教都完全不同；而且，如果吠陀教導我們的僅是這一單一神教（henotheism）的階段——這個階段必然在我們於希臘、羅馬及其他地方所見的更高度組織化的多神教階段之前出現——那麼對吠陀的研究也就不會是徒勞的了。

誠然，從我們對「詩」一詞的理解而言，吠陀的詩歌既不美麗，亦不甚深奧；然而它是富有教益的。當我們看到那兩尊天地的巨大幽靈在吠陀宗教的背景中顯現，一度發揮其影響力，隨後在年輕且更活躍的提婆之光中消散時，我們學到了一堂課——這堂課值得學習，也幾乎無法在他處習得——即諸神如何被創造與遺忘的功課。無限者（*the Beyond or the Infinite*）曾以種種不同的名字被命名，目的是將其拉近於人心，使其在一時間得以理解；直到名字一個個皆證明無濟於事之際，一尊無名之神方才被感受為最足以應答人心中那不安的渴慕。

如果我將翻譯之前提及的那首獻給河流的讚歌。如果河流要被稱為神祇的話，它們屬於陸地神祇的範疇。但我之所以特別挑出這首讚歌，與其說是因為它對神靈生成過程投射新的光芒，不如說是因為它有助於賦予我們關於古代吠陀詩人及其周遭環境所形成的模糊觀念以某種真實性。被祈求的河流，如我們將看到的，乃是旁遮普的實際河流，而這首詩表現出的地理視野遠比我們從一位純粹的鄉間詩人所預期的要寬闊得多。* 1. 「願詩人在毗瓦斯瓦特（Vivasvat）的住所此處宣告，諸水啊，汝等無與倫比的偉大。七個七個，它們在三條水道中流出，然而辛杜河（Sindhu，印度河）以其力量超越所有其他遊蕩的河流。」
2. 「當汝奔赴競賽時，婆樓那為汝掘鑿道路使汝行走。汝前行至……」毗毘斯瓦特（Vivasvat）是太陽的名稱，而毗毘斯瓦特的座位或家園幾乎不可能是除了大地之外的任何東西，蓋作為太陽之家，或在更特殊的意義上，即舉行祭祀的地方。

我之前曾將 yat vagan abhi adrava-tvam 譯為「當汝為奪取獎品而奔馳時」。格拉斯曼的譯法相似，「當汝，辛杜河啊，奔馳以奪取戰役之獎品時」，而路德維希則寫道：「當汝，辛杜河啊，流向更大的力量時。」Vagan 與 vegeo、vigeo、vigil、wacker 有關（參見 Curtius，《語言學基礎》，第 150 號），是吠陀中許多困難詞彙之一，其總體含義或可推測，但在許多地方仍未能確定。Vagan 出現頻繁，既有單數形式，亦有複數形式，其某些含義是相當清楚的。彼得堡詞典列舉了下列含義——迅捷、賽跑、賽跑獎品、收穫、財寶、賽馬等。此處我們立即察覺到追溯所有這些含義的困難之處——將之回溯至某一單一的根本含義。在此處，「當汝在地上險峭的山脊，當汝為所有流動之溪流的先鋒之主時」。
3. 「音聲升騰至天地之上；她以光輝激動她無盡的力量。*如同雲雨，驟雨轟然迸發，當辛杜河（Sindhu）如公牛般咆哮而至。

4. 「啊辛杜河（Sindhu），眾河川如同泌乳的母牛奔向其幼兒般，向汝流來。汝如戰場中的君王領導兩翼，當汝抵達這些奔騰河流的前陣之時，5. 『接納吧恆河（Ganga）、閻摩那河（Yamuna）、薩拉斯瓦蒂河（Sarasvati）、蘇圖德里河（Sutudri）、帕魯什尼河（Parushni），其源共歸一處。雖然有可能從力量、爭鬥、競賽、競逐等義開始——無論是友善或好戰之意——隨後進展到競賽或戰爭中所獲之物，即掠奪品、寶藏，最後以更廣泛的意義採用 vaga 以指獲取物、貨物，甚至作為禮物贈予之物。我們在希臘文 ἀθλος（aethlos，競賽以求獎賞）與 ἆθλον（aethlon，競賽的獎品、酬勞、禮物）中看到類似的意義轉移，而其複數形 τὰ ἆθλα（ta aethla）又復指競賽，甚至指戰鬥之地。吠陀梵文 vagambhara 實際上可以用 ἀθλοφάβος（aethlophabs）來翻譯，vagasati 可用 ἀθλοδύτη（aethlodute）來翻譯。從戰鬥到獎賞的這種意義轉移見於如下段落：《梨俱吠陀》第六卷第 45 頌第 12 句，vagam indra jravayyan tvaya meshma hilam dhanam，『啊因陀羅，願我們藉汝之力贏得榮耀的戰鬥——所獻之獎賞』（參照 ἀθλοδύτης）。」
梨俱吠陀第八卷第十九讚第十八頌：te it va^ebhi>^ ^"gyu/^ mahat dhdnam，「他們藉由戰役贏得了偉大的財富。」

我們要正確理解這首詩句，所需要的是如此處一樣，有著朝向 vā́j（複數）運動的段落。這類段落為數不多；例如 X. 53. 8，「atra ahám yé ajan ájeva jívan vayám út táreme abhí vā́jin」，意為「讓我們離開這裡那些不幸的人（死者），讓我們奮起走向幸運的勞苦。」當人們說信度河（Sindhu）朝向她的 vā́j 奔流時，大概也沒有更多的意思，也就是說朝向她的奮鬥、她的戰鬥、她與其他河流在山脈間的競逐。我們在 II. 11. 4 發現 jubhram jushman；以及 IV. 17. 12 中的 iyati 與 jushman。
見 Muir《梵文文獻》第五卷第 344 頁。
我必須坦誠地指出，所提供的英文原文似乎已嚴重損壞或誤掃，充滿了斷裂的字符、隨意的符號與無法解讀的標記。這段文本無法被合理地翻譯成繁體中文。 建議您： 1. 確認原始掃描或文本檔案的品質 2. 提供清晰可讀的英文原文 3. 如有紙本原著，請重新掃描或逐字輸入 待您提供可讀的英文原文後，我將完整執行翻譯任務。
6. 「最初汝與圖什塔瑪（Tushthama）同行，伴著蘇撒圖（Susartu）、拉薩河（Rasa [Ragha, Araxes]？），與斯維特河同往——啊，辛德（Sindhu），與庫巴河（Kubha，科芬河、喀布爾河）至戈馬提河（Gomati，戈馬爾河），與梅哈特努河至克魯姆河（Krumu，庫魯姆河）——汝與彼等一同前行。」
「閃爍、光明、以強大的光輝，她載著水流跨越平原——不可征服的信度河（Sindhu），最迅疾者中最迅疾——如同美麗的母馬——是令人賞心悅目的景象。」

8. 「富有馬匹、戰車、衣衫、黃金、戰利品、羊毛與稻草，辛都河、蘇什莫河，」路德維希云，「阿西克尼河與維塔斯托河及馬魯德瓦達河，與蘇什莫河同，請聽我們，哦阿瑞基耶，」格拉斯曼云。^ † 馬魯德瓦達河，乃河流之通稱。據羅特之說，為阿克西尼河與海達斯匹河之合流前之水道；據路德維希之說，則為該河與海德羅特河匯合後之河流。濟默爾（《古印度生活》，第12頁）採納羅特之說。基珀爾在其地圖上則遵循路德維希之見解。
根據亞斯克（Y^ska），雅利安（Ar^kiy^）是韋維安‧德‧聖馬丹（Vivien de Saint-Martin）所謂由蘇萬河灌溉的地區，即美伽斯提尼斯（Megasthenes）筆下的索阿諾斯河（Soanos）流域。
§ 根據耶斯卡（Yaska）的說法，蘇須摩河（Sushom）即為印度河。維維安‧德‧聖馬丁（Vivien de Saint-Martin）將其認同為蘇萬河。齊默爾（Zimmer, 同上，第14頁）指出在阿里安（Arrian）《印度誌》第四卷第12節中，有一異文讀法將蘇阿諾斯（Soanos）作為索阿莫斯（Soamos）。

II 《德國作坊的片簡》卷一，第157頁。Vāṇinivati（梵）絕非易於理解的詞彙。因此所有譯者各持己見，莫衷一是。穆伊爾（Muir）譯為「提供營養」；齊默爾（Zimmer）譯為「擁有眾多駿馬」；路德維希（Ludwig）譯為「如同強悍的母馬」。Vāṇ（梵）無疑指強悍的馬、賽馬，然而vāṇi在《梨俱吠陀》中從未出現「母馬」之義，文本實為vāṇivati而非vāṇinivati。若vāṇi意為母馬，我們或可譯為「富於母馬者」，但此乃在svajva（擁有良駿）後之單純重複。Vāṇivati主要用於烏莎絲（Ushas）、薩拉斯瓦蒂（Sarasvati）及此處之辛度河（Sindhu）。此詞與vāebhiḥ（梵）並用，見《梨俱吠陀》I. 3. 10，若vāṇi意為母馬，將意為「透過馬而富於母馬」。我們亦見《梨俱吠陀》I. 48. 16，sam (naḥ mimikshva) vāaiḥ vāṇivati，殆難譯為「賜我等馬匹，汝乃富於母馬者」；亦見《梨俱吠陀》I. 92. 15，yukshva hi vāṇinivati aśvan，「駕馭馬匹，汝富於母馬者」。在vāṇivati出現之多數段落，被如是稱頌之女神皆被描繪為富有者，且被祈求賜予財富，故我寧取vāṇi為集合抽象名詞，如tretani（梵）之例，以「財富」之義，原指戰利品，逕將vāṇivati譯為「富有者」，此義適用於該詞出現之所有段落。
烏爾紮瓦蒂（Urvāvatī）富饒的羊毛，大概是指印度西北部以飼養羊群聞名的畜牧。參見《梨俱吠陀》第一卷第126首第7頌。

「盛產植物；」齊默爾主張「盛產水源；」路德維希將其視為專有名詞。薩亞札指出 silama 是一種植物，被製成繩索。該字義在早期已被遺忘，我們可從《雅吠陀》(Atharva-veda) III. 12. 2 看出，該處以 snnr/tavati 替代保存於《商卡亞那家庭經》(Sankhāyana Gṛhyasutras) 3, 3 的 silamavati。我認為 silama 意為稻草，不論取自何種植物皆然，這同樣適用於 jalā（住宅）、sutha（柱子）和印度河。如路德維希所推測，它可能曾是古老的地方名稱，若果真如此，它可能有助於解釋後世賦予蘇萊曼山脈的名稱。

梨俱吠陀。沙亞那（Saya^za）以無質性（nirguw^i）及類似植物來解釋之，但所指植物究竟為何則不甚明確。古瓦法（Guwafa）是一種草的名稱，因此馬杜夫日德（madhuvrzdh）可能是一種產甜汁的植物，例如甘蔗，而印度河上游以盛產甘蔗著稱；參見希望蘭‧藏（Hiouen-thsang），第二冊，第105頁。我與羅特（Roth）一致，將阿迪瓦斯特（adhivaste）理解為「她為自己穿衣」之意，正如我們可能說「河流被石南草所披覆」。穆爾（Muir）譯為「她穿越一片產生甜蜜的土地」；齊默爾（Zimmer）譯為「她以馬杜夫日德披覆自身」；路德維希（Ludwig）譯為「錫拉瑪瓦蒂河投身於蜜甜增長者之中」。178 印度能教導我們什麼？
馬匹啊，願她在競賽中為我們奪得獎品。她戰車的偉大受讚美為真正的偉大——那無法抗拒的戰車，具有自身的榮光與豐沛的力量。」*這首讚歌在我們的語義上或許聽起來不甚富詩意；然而若你願意設身處地體察作詩人的思想，你將會看出這首讚歌並非全然缺乏某些大膽而有力的觀念。

看看現代住在泰晤士河畔村莊裡的農民，你必須承認，若有人能說服自己把泰晤士河看作某種將軍——騎在眾多英國河流的前頭，領導它們進行競賽或戰役——那此人確實非同尋常。然而，在現代英格蘭旅行並對該國河系獲得全面的認識，遠比三千年前在印度旅行要容易得多，即便只在我們讚歌詩人所談及的印度那個地區旅行。他一舉涵蓋了三大河系，或如他所稱的，三大「河流軍隊」——那些從西北方流入印度河的河流，那些從東北方加入它的河流，以及遠處的恆河與朱木那河及其支流。看看地圖，你就會明白這三大軍隊的劃分是多麼清晰；但我們的詩人沒有地圖——他只有高山和敏銳的眼力來進行他的三角測量。現在，我稱讚一個人，他第一次在……。這一切都表明，若不同時為每一個字詞所賦予的意義提供充分的正當理由，僅僅透過翻譯詞語或經文是無法在吠陀學問上取得什麼進展的。
吠陀的教訓。1 79 一位詩人能夠看見那三支行進中的河流軍隊。

在那首讚歌——如果我們必須稱它為讚歌的話——中，接著令人注目的事實是：所有這些河流，無論大小，都有各自恰當的名稱。這表明文明生活已經達到了相當的進步，並且證明了佔據北方印度的各部落之間具有不小程度的凝聚力，或者用法語所謂的「團結」（solidarité）。大多數定居者都會把他們定居的河岸上的那條河稱為「河流」。當然，河流有許多種稱呼。它可能被稱為「奔流者」、「施肥者」、「咆哮者」——或者，用稍許詩意的隱喻，稱為「箭」、「馬」、「牛」、「父親」、「母親」、「守衛者」、「山的孩子」。許多河流在其流經的不同地段有著許多不同的名稱，只有當不同定居區之間的交通往來變得更加頻繁，以及當一套固定的名詞系統被認為是必要之舉時，一個國家的河流才得以被妥善地命名和登記。在我們的讚歌成為可能之前，印度已經歷了所有這一切。

現在我們必須考慮另一個在我看來最令人震驚的事實。我們這裡有一些印度河流的名稱，正如約在公元前1000年左右的某位詩人所知悉的那樣。隨後我們對印度一無所聞，直到亞歷山大時代才再次聽聞，而當我們查看印度河流的名稱時，這些名稱是由亞歷山大的同伴們盡可能地記錄下來的——他們不過是印度的陌生人——以及f * 在霍騰督人中，庫內內河（Kunene）、卡瓦甘河（Okavang）和奧蘭治河（Orange）都有高里卜（Garib）這個名稱，即「奔馳者」之意。提奧菲魯斯‧哈恩博士，開普敦，1882年7月11日。
